meta.title: Joe Kittinger: An Unsung Hero
meta.author: Ned Jensen
meta.focusQuestion: How did Joe Kittinger's personality, accomplishments, and influence make him an unsung hero of space exploration?

sections.title: Introduction
paragraphs: Space exploration has many heroes. If you were asked to name a few, you might mention Yuri Gagarin, the first person to travel into space, or Neil Armstrong, the first person to walk on the moon. Or you might name Alan Shepard, John Glenn, or Sally Ride, all well-known American astronauts. But you probably would not include Joe Kittinger on your list. He is an unsung hero.

However, many people regard Joe as a true pioneer of space exploration. Joe Kittinger was neither an ordinary space explorer nor an actual astronaut or cosmonaut. However, what Joe did probably took more courage than that of any astronaut who climbed into a space shuttle or capsule and rocketed into space. What did Joe do that was so extraordinary? Let's find out.

sections.title: Jumping Joe
paragraphs: Before humans were zooming into space in rocket-propelled capsules, Joe Kittinger was flying high-altitude balloons into the very edge of space. It was all part of a U.S. Air Force project called Project Manhigh. The project's purpose was to test what would happen to humans who spent a long time in conditions similar to those found in outer space. In June 1957, Joe made his first balloon flight into the stratosphere and reached an altitude of 29,565 meters (97,000 ft). He was aloft for almost seven hours. The information gathered from high-altitude balloon flights by Joe and others was invaluable to NASA's planning for the first human space flights.

Later, Joe was the engineer for Project Excelsior, a U.S. Air Force project to develop safe ways to escape from high-altitude aircraft and spaceships. In August 1960, a year before Russian cosmonaut Yuri Gagarin became the first person considered to travel into space, Joe Kittinger accomplished an equally incredible feat. He rode in a balloon filled with helium gas to a distance of 31,333 meters (102,800 ft) above Earth. A typical passenger jet flies at about 9,144 meters (30,000 ft) above Earth. Joe went more than three times higher than passenger jets fly—in a balloon!

Okay, so that's pretty incredible, isn't it? But get this—next, Joe stepped out of the balloon! At 31,333 meters (102,800 ft) above Earth, he stepped out of the open gondola into the emptiness of space. He wasn't going for a space walk. Instead, he jumped out and began falling back to Earth.

sections.title: The Jump
paragraphs: Before we learn what happened to Joe, let's think about conditions at 31,333 meters (102,800 ft) above Earth. At that altitude, the air is very thin, which makes it next to impossible to inhale enough oxygen with each breath. Joe carried a supply of oxygen, which allowed him to breathe comfortably. However, it is also incredibly cold that high above Earth. At the 32-kilometer (20 mi) distance Joe traveled in his balloon, he was deep into a layer of the atmosphere called the stratosphere. This layer stretches from 9.5 to 48 kilometers (about 6–30 mi) above Earth. So Joe was floating well into the stratosphere, where temperatures can be as low as −68 degrees Celsius (−90°F).

To keep himself warm in these hostile conditions, Joe wore several layers of clothing. He also needed to wear a special pressurized suit to prevent his body from exploding and his blood from boiling. The danger of this happening was caused by the low-pressure conditions of space, where very little air exists. In space, pressure inside the body is much greater than the pressure from the outside atmosphere, which pushes against the body. This can happen starting at an altitude of 18,900 meters (62,000 ft), which is known as Armstrong's Limit. Despite all the measures taken to keep Joe safe, he experienced pain in his right hand when the pressurized glove he wore developed a leak and lost pressure.

Let's continue to follow Joe in his amazing fall through the atmosphere. He was being pulled by gravity back home to Earth. As he fell, his body accelerated, reaching speeds of over 965 kilometers (600 mi) per hour. Some experts say he even may have broken the sound barrier. If so, he was the only person ever to break the sound barrier unaided or unprotected by any sort of craft for over fifty years.

After falling for 16 seconds, Joe's small, 1.8-meter (6 ft) diameter drogue parachute opened and slowed his fall. A drogue parachute is elongated and thinner than a regular parachute. This design stops the parachute from being torn apart during high-speed falls from high altitudes. Joe fell for another 4 minutes and 36 seconds before his main parachute opened, allowing him to drift slowly down to Earth. Joe had proven that a human could survive in near spacelike conditions outside a spacecraft or aircraft after an emergency escape.

sections.title: Joe Sets Another Record
paragraphs: You might ask whether Joe was really in space—technically speaking, he was not. Space really does not have a well-defined border. It's not like stepping across the border of one country into another. The atmosphere just keeps getting thinner and thinner as you move away from Earth. However, many scientists define the border of space—called the Kármán Line—as 100 kilometers (62 mi) above Earth. So it would appear that Joe didn't actually reach true outer space. Nonetheless, he was high enough to experience conditions similar to those in space. And remember, he wasn't even inside a spaceship. Joe's daring jump set an altitude record for a parachute jump that remained unbroken until 2012.

Joe was more than a guy who jumped out of balloons. He was also a highly skilled pilot with a love of flying that began early in his life. Born in 1928 in Tampa, Florida, Joe spent his early years growing up around Orlando, Florida. You might say he was obsessed with flying. He read everything he could find about flying and pilots, and he built many model airplanes.

Joe spent his teenage years hanging around a local airport and persuading pilots to give him free plane rides. By age 17, he had flown his first solo flight. Joe joined the U.S. Air Force after two years of college and soon became a test pilot, flying experimental and fighter aircraft.

In 1955, Joe went from piloting airplanes to piloting balloons. He had made several high-altitude balloon flights and balloon jumps before his record-setting flight and jump in 1960. In one of those earlier jumps, Joe's drogue chute opened only 2 seconds after he jumped from the gondola, instead of after 16 seconds as it was supposed to. A cord from the chute wrapped around his neck and sent him into a near-fatal downward spiral. Joe became unconscious during the violent spinning. But luckily, his safety chute opened automatically and slowed his fall, allowing him to float safely down to Earth.

You might think that this experience would have caused Joe to think about changing his career, but it didn't. He stuck with the project and made other flights, which led to his record-setting balloon flight and jump.

After setting the world record for his high-altitude parachute jump, Joe went to Vietnam and flew combat missions. He served three combat tours during the war and flew a record-breaking 483 flight missions. In 1972, on his 483rd flight, his luck ran out when he was shot down. That became his last flight in combat.

Joe was able to eject from his crashing jet and parachute safely to Earth. Unfortunately, he landed behind enemy lines, 30 miles northwest of Hanoi, North Vietnam, and was captured immediately. For the next 11 months, Joe was a prisoner of war held by his captors in the infamous "Hanoi Hilton" prison. Joe was placed in solitary confinement for 30 days. During his time as a prisoner, Joe occupied his mind by planning every detail of a balloon flight around the world—a flight that no human had accomplished.

sections.title: More Records for Joe
paragraphs: After retiring from the Air Force in 1978, Joe continued his work in aeronautics. He also added to his other record-setting accomplishments by flying lighter-than-air balloons. He entered many competitions around the United States. Five years after his retirement from the Air Force, Joe set yet another record. He flew a helium-filled balloon from Las Vegas, Nevada, to Franklinville, New York—a distance of 3,220 kilometers (2,000 mi). It took him 72 hours to fly the record-setting distance.

In 1984, at the age of 56, Joe decided to go for another record. No one had flown a balloon alone across the Atlantic Ocean—all the more reason for Joe to try it. On September 14, 1984, he took off from the state of Maine and drifted eastward. Almost 84 hours and 5,633 kilometers (3,500 mi) later, he landed his balloon in Italy. Joe had set another record.

sections.title: Conclusion
paragraphs: The dictionary defines a hero as someone who commits an act of bravery or who shows great courage when undertaking a task. Based on this definition, Joe is certainly a hero. Many heroes in fields like exploration become well known, with their names appearing in the news and in textbooks. Joe Kittinger is not a name that comes to mind in connection with space exploration. In this sense, Joe is an unsung hero. His early work with high-altitude balloons was not undertaken for the purpose of setting records, but rather for the benefit of pilots and astronauts.

Despite being relatively unknown to the general public, Joe was recognized for his outstanding accomplishments. U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower awarded him the Harmon Trophy after Joe set the record for a high-altitude jump. Joe received many other awards and honors for his military, ballooning, and parachuting achievements, including the Silver Star, Distinguished Flying Cross, and Purple Heart. Joe is a man of extraordinary courage and bravery who made important contributions to the field of space exploration.

questions.question: What was this book mainly about?
questions.evidence: But you probably would not include Joe Kittinger on your list. He is an unsung hero. However, many people regard Joe as a true pioneer of space exploration.
choices: A future leader in the race to explore deep space
How to train to live and work in outer space
A famous astronaut who walked on the moon
A little-known pioneer of space exploration
questions.explanation: The book is mainly about Joe Kittinger, a little-known (unsung) pioneer of space exploration who made critical contributions through high-altitude balloon flights and jumps.

questions.question: What was the purpose of Project Manhigh?
questions.evidence: The project's purpose was to test what would happen to humans who spent a long time in conditions similar to those found in outer space.
choices: To find out what type of spacesuit would work best
To see how high a person could go without oxygen
To test conditions similar to those in outer space
To try out new equipment designed for travel to the moon
questions.explanation: Project Manhigh's purpose was to test what would happen to humans who spent a long time in conditions similar to those found in outer space.

questions.question: What is Armstrong's Limit, mentioned in the book?
questions.evidence: This can happen starting at an altitude of 18,900 meters (62,000 ft), which is known as Armstrong's Limit.
choices: The border of space, 100 km above Earth
The altitude where blood can boil from low pressure
The speed at which a parachute must open
The highest point a passenger jet can fly
questions.explanation: Armstrong's Limit is the altitude (18,900 meters / 62,000 ft) where the outside atmospheric pressure is so low that pressure inside the body is much greater, which can cause blood to boil.

questions.question: What did Joe do as a child that showed his love of flying?
questions.evidence: You might say he was obsessed with flying. He read everything he could find about flying and pilots, and he built many model airplanes.
choices: He competed in model airplane building contests
He took his first solo flight before age 17
He learned to parachute from a local airport tower
He read about flying and built model airplanes
questions.explanation: As a child, Joe was obsessed with flying — he read everything he could find about flying and pilots, and he built many model airplanes.

questions.question: What made Joe's 1960 balloon flight and jump so impressive?
questions.evidence: He rode in a balloon filled with helium gas to a distance of 31,333 meters (102,800 ft) above Earth. A typical passenger jet flies at about 9,144 meters (30,000 ft) above Earth. Joe went more than three times higher than passenger jets fly—in a balloon!
choices: He set an altitude record of 102,800 ft
He set a speed record of over 600 mph
He was the first person to walk in space
He broke the sound barrier during free fall
questions.explanation: Joe's 1960 flight was impressive because he reached an altitude of 102,800 ft — more than three times higher than passenger jets fly — and then jumped from the balloon.

questions.question: What happened when Joe stepped out of the gondola at 102,800 ft?
questions.evidence: At that altitude, the air is very thin, which makes it next to impossible to inhale enough oxygen with each breath.
choices: The air was thin — nearly impossible to breathe
He floated in space because there was no gravity
He immediately passed out from lack of oxygen
His suit exploded from the pressure difference
questions.explanation: At 102,800 ft, the air was so thin that it was next to impossible to inhale enough oxygen. Joe carried a supply of oxygen to breathe comfortably.

questions.question: What was the purpose of the drogue parachute?
questions.evidence: A drogue parachute is elongated and thinner than a regular parachute. This design stops the parachute from being torn apart during high-speed falls from high altitudes.
choices: To send a signal to the ground team
To keep Joe warm during the long fall
To help Joe steer toward a landing zone
To slow his fall before the main chute opened
questions.explanation: The drogue parachute was elongated and thinner than a regular parachute. Its purpose was to stop the parachute from being torn apart during high-speed falls and to slow Joe's fall before the main parachute opened.

questions.question: What record did Joe set during the Vietnam War?
questions.evidence: He served three combat tours during the war and flew a record-breaking 483 flight missions.
choices: He became the fastest pilot in the U.S. Air Force
He completed the most parachute rescues in combat history
He flew a record-breaking 483 combat flight missions
He set a record for the longest solo combat flight
questions.explanation: During the Vietnam War, Joe served three combat tours and flew a record-breaking 483 flight missions before being shot down on his 483rd flight.

questions.question: How did Joe occupy his mind during his 11 months as a prisoner of war?
questions.evidence: During his time as a prisoner, Joe occupied his mind by planning every detail of a balloon flight around the world—a flight that no human had accomplished.
choices: He wrote a book about his flying experiences
He practiced exercises in his cell every day
He taught other prisoners to fly balloons
He planned a balloon flight around the world
questions.explanation: During his 11 months as a POW, Joe occupied his mind by planning every detail of a balloon flight around the world — something no human had accomplished.

questions.question: What record did Joe set in 1984?
questions.evidence: On September 14, 1984, he took off from the state of Maine and drifted eastward. Almost 84 hours and 5,633 kilometers (3,500 mi) later, he landed his balloon in Italy. Joe had set another record.
choices: He flew a balloon at the fastest speed ever
He flew a balloon alone across the Atlantic
He set a new distance record for balloons
He became the oldest person to fly a balloon
questions.explanation: In 1984, at age 56, Joe became the first person to fly a balloon alone across the Atlantic Ocean, traveling from Maine to Italy in almost 84 hours.

questions.question: Do you think Joe Kittinger deserves to be called a hero of space exploration, alongside famous astronauts like Neil Armstrong and John Glenn? Use evidence from the text to support your answer.
questions.explanation: Think about what Joe accomplished and how his work helped astronauts and pilots. Does his contribution make him a hero, even though he is not as well known?
opinionPrompts: What does the dictionary definition of a hero say, according to the book?
What specific accomplishments and contributions did Joe make to space exploration?
Why is Joe called an 'unsung hero' rather than a famous one? What evidence supports your opinion?

characterAnalysis.quote: "His early work with high-altitude balloons was not undertaken for the purpose of setting records, but rather for the benefit of pilots and astronauts." —from the text
characterAnalysis.quotePrompt: How does this quote reflect Joe's personality? How did his personality lead to his accomplishments? How did his accomplishments influence the world?

Personality.prompt: What qualities made Joe Kittinger unique?
items: Obsessed with flying from a young age — read everything about flying and built model airplanes
Extraordinarily courageous — jumped from 102,800 ft into the near-vacuum of space
Resilient and persistent — stuck with Project Excelsior even after a near-fatal accident
Determined — endured hostile conditions, 30 days of solitary confinement, and 11 months as a POW
Selfless — his work was done for the benefit of pilots and astronauts, not for fame
Project Manhigh (1957): First balloon flight into the stratosphere, reaching 97,000 ft — data was invaluable to NASA
Project Excelsior (1960): Rode a balloon to 102,800 ft and jumped — set an altitude record that stood for 52 years
Accelerated to over 600 mph in free fall — may have been the first to break the sound barrier unaided
Flew 483 combat missions in Vietnam before being shot down and captured as a POW
Flew a balloon 2,000 miles from Las Vegas to New York in 72 hours (1983)
First person to fly a balloon alone across the Atlantic Ocean (1984) — at age 56

Influence.prompt: How did Joe Kittinger affect the world?
items: Data from his high-altitude balloon flights was invaluable to NASA's planning for the first human space flights
Proved that humans could survive in near-spacelike conditions outside a spacecraft after emergency escape
Helped develop safe escape methods for high-altitude aircraft and spaceships through Project Excelsior
Worked with Felix Baumgartner for years to prepare for the 2012 jump that broke Joe's altitude record
Received the Harmon Trophy from President Eisenhower, plus the Silver Star, Distinguished Flying Cross, and Purple Heart
Inspired future generations by showing extraordinary courage as an 'unsung hero' of space exploration

sequenceEvents.event: Joe Kittinger was born in 1928 in Tampa, Florida, and grew up around Orlando. As a child, he was obsessed with flying — he read everything about aviation and built model airplanes.

After college and joining the U.S. Air Force as a test pilot, Joe switched from airplanes to balloons in 1955. In June 1957, he made his first balloon flight into the stratosphere for Project Manhigh, reaching 97,000 ft.
Joe rode a helium balloon to 102,800 ft and jumped from the open gondola, accelerating to over 600 mph before his parachute opened. He set an altitude record that stood unbroken for 52 years.
Joe served three combat tours in Vietnam, flying a record-breaking 483 missions. On his 483rd flight in 1972, he was shot down, captured, and held as a POW for 11 months in the 'Hanoi Hilton.'

After retiring from the Air Force in 1978, Joe continued in aeronautics. He set a balloon distance record (Las Vegas to New York, 72 hours) and became the first person to fly a balloon alone across the Atlantic (Maine to Italy, 84 hours) at age 56.

Joe received the Harmon Trophy from President Eisenhower, plus the Silver Star, Distinguished Flying Cross, and Purple Heart. He worked with Felix Baumgartner for the 2012 jump and is remembered as an unsung hero of space exploration.

grammar.title: Hyphenated Compound Adjectives
grammar.explanation: A hyphenated compound adjective is two or more words joined by a hyphen to describe a noun together. The hyphen shows that the words work as a single describing unit. For example, in 'high-altitude balloons,' the words 'high' and 'altitude' are joined with a hyphen because they together describe the type of balloons.
rules: A hyphenated compound adjective = two or more words joined by a hyphen (-) that describe a noun
The hyphen prevents confusion: 'small balloon jump' could mean a small jump or a small balloon, but 'high-altitude jump' clearly means a jump at high altitude
Compound adjectives are usually hyphenated when they come BEFORE the noun they describe: 'a near-fatal accident' (before 'accident')
Common patterns in this book: number + noun (high-altitude), adjective + participle (rocket-propelled), adjective + noun (near-fatal)
Examples from the book: 'high-altitude balloons,' 'rocket-propelled capsules,' 'near-fatal downward spiral,' 'record-setting flight,' 'pressurized suit'

fillInBlanks.sentence: Joe flew _____ balloons into the very edge of space.
fillInBlanks.hint: These balloons flew very high above Earth.

fillInBlanks.sentence: A cord from the chute sent Joe into a _____ downward spiral.
Joe was flying in _____ capsules before humans went to space.
fillInBlanks.hint: The capsules were pushed by rockets.

fillInBlanks.sentence: Joe needed a special _____ suit to survive at extreme heights.
fillInBlanks.hint: The suit maintained the right air pressure around his body.

fillInBlanks.sentence: A drogue parachute is elongated and _____ than a regular parachute.
fillInBlanks.hint: Compare the drogue chute's width to a regular one.

fillInBlanks.sentence: Joe stuck with the project and made his _____ balloon flight in 1960.
fillInBlanks.hint: This flight set a new world record.

exercises.sentence: Before humans were zooming into space in rocket-propelled capsules, Joe Kittinger was flying high-altitude balloons.
A cord from the chute wrapped around his neck and sent him into a near-fatal downward spiral.
In one of those earlier jumps, Joe's drogue chute opened only 2 seconds after he jumped from the gondola.
exercises.note: This sentence has no hyphenated compound adjective — 'drogue' is a single-word adjective describing 'chute.'

exercises.sentence: Joe was obsessed with flying and built many _____ airplanes.
exercises.explanation: 'Model' is used as a single-word adjective here — no hyphen is needed. 'Model airplanes' = airplanes that are small copies of real ones.

exercises.sentence: Joe set a _____ distance by flying a balloon from Las Vegas to New York.
exercises.explanation: 'Record-breaking' is a hyphenated compound adjective because 'record' and 'breaking' work together to describe 'distance.' The hyphen joins them before the noun.

exercises.sentence: Joe's _____ jump in 1960 set an altitude record that stood for 52 years.
exercises.explanation: 'High-altitude' is a hyphenated compound adjective describing 'jump.' Both words together modify the noun, so they are joined by a hyphen.

exercises.sentence: Joe wore a (pressurized suit) to prevent his body from exploding.
exercises.explanation: 'Pressurized' is a single-word adjective modifying 'suit.' No hyphen is needed because it is just one word, not a compound.

exercises.sentence: Joe's (near fatal) accident happened when the chute cord wrapped around his neck.
exercises.explanation: 'Near-fatal' is a hyphenated compound adjective. 'Near' and 'fatal' work together to describe 'accident,' so they are joined with a hyphen.

wordWork.title: Reading Numbers Aloud
wordWork.explanation: When we read large numbers aloud, we use place-value words like thousand, million, and hundred. Commas help us group digits into chunks. In '102,800,' the comma separates the thousands group (102) from the ones group (800). We read it as 'one hundred two thousand, eight hundred.' Negative numbers are read by saying 'negative' or 'below zero' before the number.
rules: Read each group of three digits (separated by commas) as its own mini-number, then add the place-value word
For decimals and negative numbers: −68°C → 'negative sixty-eight degrees Celsius'
For large distances: 5,633 → 'five thousand, six hundred thirty-three'

intro.title: An Unsung Hero
intro.content: The book calls Joe an UNSUNG HERO — meaning he did amazing things but isn't famous like Neil Armstrong or John Glenn. WHY isn't he as well known? Keep reading to find out!

intro.title: Cosmonaut
intro.content: A COSMONAUT is a Russian space traveler. The American word is ASTRONAUT. Joe was NEITHER — but what he did took MORE courage than climbing into a rocket!

jumpingJoe.title: Project Manhigh & Project Excelsior
jumpingJoe.content: PROJECT MANHIGH tested what space-like conditions do to humans. Joe reached 97,000 ft in 1957! Then PROJECT EXCELSIOR aimed to develop safe escape methods. Joe went to 102,800 ft — over 3x higher than jets fly — in a BALLOON!

jumpingJoe.title: Altitude, Invaluable, Stratosphere
jumpingJoe.content: ALTITUDE = height above Earth. Joe's data was INVALUABLE (priceless!) to NASA. The STRATOSPHERE is 6–30 miles above Earth — Joe floated right into it!

theJump.title: Extreme Conditions & Armstrong's Limit
theJump.content: At 102,800 ft: air so THIN you can't breathe, temps as low as −68°C (−90°F), and HOSTILE conditions! ARMSTRONG'S LIMIT (62,000 ft) is where pressure is so low that BLOOD CAN BOIL! Joe wore a pressurized suit to survive.

theJump.title: Accelerated, Elongated, Gondola
theJump.content: Joe's body ACCELERATED to 600+ mph! His DROGUE chute was ELONGATED (stretched out and thin) so it wouldn't tear apart. He stepped from the GONDOLA — the boat-like enclosure hanging from the balloon.

theJump.title: Hyphenated Compound Adjective
theJump.content: 'His body ACCELERATED, reaching speeds of over 965 km per hour.' Joe may have broken the sound barrier — the only person to do so UNAIDED for over 50 YEARS!

joeSetsAnotherRecord.title: Joe's Early Life & Vietnam
joeSetsAnotherRecord.content: Joe was OBSESSED with flying as a kid! He hung around airports as a teenager, flew solo at 17, then joined the Air Force. He had a NEAR-FATAL accident when a chute cord wrapped around his neck — but he STUCK WITH the project! Later, he flew 483 combat missions in Vietnam and was a POW for 11 months in SOLITARY CONFINEMENT for 30 days!

joeSetsAnotherRecord.title: Obsessed & Near-Fatal
joeSetsAnotherRecord.content: OBSESSED = completely occupied with thinking about something. Joe was obsessed with FLYING! NEAR-FATAL = almost resulting in death. Joe's chute accident was near-fatal, but he didn't quit!

afterTheJump.title: More Records After Retirement
afterTheJump.content: After retiring in 1978, Joe continued in AERONAUTICS! At age 56, he flew a balloon ALONE across the ATLANTIC — 3,500 miles in 84 hours! He just kept setting records!

afterTheJump.title: Aeronautics
afterTheJump.content: AERONAUTICS = the work or study of flight. Joe continued his work in aeronautics long after his famous jump!

legacy.title: Joe's Legacy as an Unsung Hero
legacy.content: A HERO shows great courage. Joe's work helped pilots and astronauts — but his name isn't in textbooks like Armstrong's! He received the HARMON TROPHY from President Eisenhower, plus the Silver Star, Distinguished Flying Cross, and Purple Heart. Joe is an UNSUNG HERO!
